perspective: Holistic report quality: outcome correctness, safety, audience fit, and usability

scoring_rules.description: Weighted average must be >= 4.0. Hard-fail if RPT-001 or RPT-003 < 4. Audience gate if RPT-004 or RPT-005 < 3.
scoring_rules.overall_formula: sum(score_i * weight_pct_i) where weight_pct_i is the dimension's percentage weight

criteria.description: Data correctness & internal consistency — all text and visuals accurately reflect the underlying data; no contradictions between sections

scoring_guide.1: Clear contradictions or incorrect claims — text says 'improving' when data is declining; hero contradicts goals
scoring_guide.2: Significant mismatches in one section; loose phrasing that misleads about data state
scoring_guide.3: Mostly accurate with minor mismatches or imprecise phrasing that doesn't truly mislead
scoring_guide.4: All claims accurate; only cosmetic inconsistencies in tone or word choice
scoring_guide.5: All text and visuals consistently match the data and each other; zero contradictions or misstatements

fail_examples: Hero says 'strong recovery week' but readiness declined from 75 to 40 over 7 days
Graph shows RHR trending down but text says 'your RHR has been rising'
Dashboard says 'great sleep' while insights section shows 4.5h average sleep this week
pass_examples: Readiness dropped 30 points and hero correctly reads 'Recovery has taken a hit — rest is the call today'
RHR trending up in graph and text reads 'Heart rate has been running a little high this week'
All sections — hero, banner, goals, nudges — reflect the same story from the same data

criteria.description: Goal alignment & appropriateness — recommended goals and daily moves match the user's situation, constraints, fitness level, and current risk

scoring_guide.1: Goals are inappropriate, unsafe, or clearly misaligned — intense running goal for injured or overtrained user
scoring_guide.2: Goals are generic and partially misaligned with persona constraints or current data
scoring_guide.3: Goals are generic but not harmful or obviously unrealistic for the user
scoring_guide.4: Goals are mostly tailored and realistic with one minor misalignment
scoring_guide.5: Goals are tailored, realistic, and clearly linked to the user's current state, constraints, and long-term aims

fail_examples: Goal suggests 10,000 steps and a 5K run for a user with readiness=18 and 3h sleep
Step goal set to maximum when user has been consistently overtrained for 5 days
Sleep goal targets 9h for a new parent who structurally cannot sleep more than 5h
pass_examples: Overtrained user gets: 'Today's goal: gentle movement only. Even 1,500 steps counts.'
Sleep-deprived user: step goal auto-reduced, primary goal reframed as 'rest before 10pm'
Injured user: no running suggestion; 'light stretching or a slow walk — whatever feels right'

criteria.description: Risk handling & safety — report never encourages unsafe behavior; appropriately escalates when risk signals are high; medical framing is cautious and non-diagnostic

scoring_guide.1: Encourages high-intensity activity or downplays serious risk signals; dangerous recommendation present
scoring_guide.2: Under-emphasizes risk in a way that could lead to harm; missing escalation language when warranted
scoring_guide.3: Some risk under-emphasized but no clearly dangerous recommendation present
scoring_guide.4: All risk states correctly reflected; no unsafe nudges; escalation present when needed
scoring_guide.5: Risk signals correctly identified; no unsafe encouragement; calm escalation language ('worth sharing with your doctor') where appropriate; no diagnostic claims

fail_examples: Readiness=12, sleep=2h, stress=critical: 'Push yourself today — your body can handle it!'
RHR 40% above baseline for 3 days: report says 'heart rate looks a bit high, no action needed'
Overtraining signals for 6 days but report still sets aggressive training goals
pass_examples: Readiness=12: 'Very low recovery. Rest is the only right call today. No goals other than sleep tonight.'
Sustained RHR elevation: 'Patterns like this are worth mentioning to your doctor, especially if you feel unwell.'
High stress + low sleep for 5 days: report pivots all goals to recovery; no training language

criteria.description: Audience fit — layperson clarity: non-technical user can read the report, understand their status, and know what to do without domain knowledge

scoring_guide.1: Relies heavily on jargon or numbers; a typical user cannot interpret or act on the report
scoring_guide.2: Some accessible language but key sections use unexplained terms or abstract advice
scoring_guide.3: Generally understandable; some jargon or dense text may confuse a non-expert
scoring_guide.4: Mostly plain language; user gets 1-3 clear actions; only one minor phrasing could be simpler
scoring_guide.5: A layperson instantly grasps status, why it matters, and next steps without technical knowledge

fail_examples: 'Your HRV RMSSD dropped 18ms below your 28-day baseline, indicating parasympathetic suppression'
'Optimize zone 2 distribution and increase sleep latency efficiency to improve readiness'
Three paragraphs of data before any clear action or human outcome
pass_examples: 'Your heart had a tough night. A calm, easy day helps it reset.' + 'Try: short walk after dinner'
'Sleep has been shorter than usual. Even one extra hour tonight makes a real difference.'
Main screen shows: status in one sentence, why in one sentence, do-this in one sentence

criteria.description: Audience fit — professional transparency: a health or technical professional can see how conclusions were reached and verify they are reasonable

scoring_guide.1: Conclusions are opaque; no data visible; appear to overstate what the inputs support
scoring_guide.2: Some data visible but traceability from data to recommendations is only partially clear
scoring_guide.3: Key inputs are named; professional can broadly verify but not fully audit the reasoning
scoring_guide.4: Data signals are visible and reasoning is clearly stated; narrative doesn't overclaim
scoring_guide.5: Professional can inspect the rationale, see which signals drove the conclusion, and verify narrative-data alignment

fail_examples: Report says 'recovery is low' with no indication of which signals (sleep? HRV? RHR?) drove it
Recommendation says 'take it easy' but there's no way to see the data behind the call
Trend claim made with no supporting data visible anywhere in the report
pass_examples: 'Recovery signal is lower than usual — mainly driven by shorter sleep (5h vs your typical 7h) and slightly elevated resting heart rate'
Detail view shows 7-day RHR trend, sleep hours, and HRV range that a clinician could review
Narrative says 'trending up' and the banded graph clearly shows an upward slope over the same window

criteria.description: Progressive detail & explainability on click — users can drill into any element for a plain explanation; layered from summary to details without forcing complexity on casual users

scoring_guide.1: No way to get more explanation; user is either stuck at surface summary or dumped into raw graphs
scoring_guide.2: Some detail available but navigation is unclear or explanations skip the plain-language step
scoring_guide.3: Detail path exists but is uneven — some cards explain well, others don't
scoring_guide.4: Interactive elements are discoverable; first expansion is clear; second step leads to graphs
scoring_guide.5: Three clear layers: main summary → plain 'why' explanation → data graphs and technical detail. Lay users stop at layer 1 or 2; professionals can go to layer 3

fail_examples: Tapping 'Heart Mode: Caution' opens a raw HRV chart with no explanation
No tap targets anywhere — report is static with no drill-down
'Learn more' goes straight to a page of bpm numbers with no context
pass_examples: Tap 'Caution' → 'We call this Caution because sleep has been short for 3 nights and stress is higher than usual' → 'View your 7-day trend'
Every card has a chevron; first tap gives a one-sentence plain explanation; second tap shows graph
Layer 1 for Sarah: 'Take it easy today.' Layer 2: 'Why? Your sleep was short and your heart signals are elevated.' Layer 3: 7-day chart

criteria.description: Graph clarity & text-graph alignment — visuals are interpretable by non-experts and consistent with the text narrative

scoring_guide.1: Graphs are confusing, unlabeled, or clearly contradict the text
scoring_guide.2: Graphs are technically correct but under-explained; text and graph tell different stories
scoring_guide.3: Graphs are correct and labeled but require domain knowledge to interpret
scoring_guide.4: Graphs are clear and mostly reinforce the text; one minor mismatch or labeling gap
scoring_guide.5: Graphs are simple, labeled in plain language, and clearly reinforce the written story; a layperson can see the pattern described

fail_examples: Text says 'RHR has been trending up' but the graph shows a flat or declining line
Graph has no axis labels; values shown with no units or reference bands
Trend described as 'slight' in text but graph shows a dramatic spike
pass_examples: Banded bar chart shows sleep hours with a green 'target' band; text reads 'You've been below your target band 4 of 7 nights'
RHR line chart with 'your typical range' shaded in grey; text reads 'this week ran a little above your usual range'
Simple weekly dots for readiness score with text reading 'sharp drop on day 5 — that's the crash day'

criteria.description: Focus & prioritization — report surfaces the 1-2 most important issues immediately; secondary details stay in supporting roles

scoring_guide.1: Important issues are buried or equal in visual weight to minor metrics
scoring_guide.2: Main issue is present but shares too much space with secondary points
scoring_guide.3: Main issue is visible but takes work to identify; too many equally-weighted items
scoring_guide.4: Clear primary issue highlighted with secondary context clearly subordinated
scoring_guide.5: User immediately sees the top 1-2 issues; everything else is clearly secondary or detail

fail_examples: Sleep debt of 3h is listed 5th after step count, active calories, heart rate variability, and zone breakdown
Bad day with readiness=15 shows 8 different cards at equal visual weight
Critical recovery signal buried inside a 'See your details' section
pass_examples: Sleep debt leads the report on a bad night; steps and other metrics are clearly secondary
On a crash day: one large card says 'Rest is the call today' with a single action; everything else collapses
On a good day: achievement card leads; recovery context is one supporting line below

criteria.description: Tone, empathy & motivation — message is emotionally appropriate, supportive, and motivating; avoids blame; supports autonomy

scoring_guide.1: Critical, shaming, or dismissive tone; likely to make users feel worse or defensive
scoring_guide.2: Neutral but cold; misses the emotional context; tone doesn't match the user's situation
scoring_guide.3: Neutral, not harmful, but generic and impersonal; no warmth or acknowledgment
scoring_guide.4: Warm and respectful; one missed opportunity for empathy or specificity
scoring_guide.5: Warm, respectful, and motivating; acknowledges effort; offers hopeful and realistic steps; supports autonomy through suggestion not command

fail_examples: Crashed day: 'You have not met your goals this week. Try harder tomorrow.'
Post-illness: 'Your metrics are below average. You should be exercising more.'
Commands instead of invites: 'You must sleep 8 hours. You must reduce stress.'
pass_examples: Crashed day: 'Rough week — your body worked hard. Rest today is doing real work, even if it doesn't feel like it.'
Post-illness: 'Coming back from being unwell takes time. Your data will catch up — don't rush it.'
Suggestion framing: 'A short walk could help, even just around the block.' not 'You need to walk.'

criteria.description: Brevity & cognitive load — report is concise enough to be usable when the user is tired or stressed; on bad days, doesn't pile on

scoring_guide.1: Overly long, dense, or cluttered; likely to overwhelm or be skipped entirely
scoring_guide.2: Noticeably wordy or repetitive; bad-day reports list too many negatives without structure
scoring_guide.3: A bit wordy or repetitive but still digestible with effort
scoring_guide.4: Concise with one section that could be trimmed; bad-day reports are compassionately brief
scoring_guide.5: Concise and focused; 1-3 main points with brief explanation; bad days get shorter not longer reports

fail_examples: Bad day report: 8 separate issues listed with equal weight over 4 scrollable cards
Recovery narrative is 5 sentences when the situation calls for one clear action
Same insight repeated in hero, banner, and goals without adding new information
pass_examples: Bad day: one card, one action, one sentence of context — report is shorter than a good day
Good day: slightly more detail because user has capacity to engage with it
Guidance text: 2 sentences max; action item: 1 concrete thing

criteria.description: Breathing feature coherence — breathing/breath-volume usage is reflected correctly; no praise for habits that don't exist; no overclaiming correlation benefits

scoring_guide.1: Frequent contradictions — praises breathing habit for a user who hasn't used it; or claims breathing always fixes stress
scoring_guide.2: Some mismatch in emphasis or overclaiming about breathing benefits
scoring_guide.3: Technically correct but breathing messaging doesn't match usage patterns
scoring_guide.4: Breathing messages align with actual behavior; correlation claims are appropriately hedged
scoring_guide.5: Breathing usage, messaging, and correlation claims are coherent and cautious; goals are sized for the user's actual habit

fail_examples: User has never used breathing feature: 'Great job keeping up your breathing practice this week!'
'Breathing always reduces your stress — it's the single best tool you have' (overclaim)
Long-time user gets 'Try breathing for the first time — just 2 minutes' (wrong onboarding language)
pass_examples: New user: 'Breathing exercises can help with moments like this — even 2 minutes makes a difference for some people'
Regular user with strong correlation: 'On days you've used breathing, your calm signal tends to recover faster'
No usage this week: no breathing mentions in the report at all
